text.title: bundle-vs-split-tools Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for bundle-vs-split-tools. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: If tool count < 20, always default to split. If 20–25, split unless tools are extremely repetitive (e.g., CRUD on 10 entities = 40 tools). If > 25, consider bundling only when modes share audience, argument shape, and training-data prior. If bundling reduces quality (measured on a 50-task eval), revert to split or split the offending bundle.
rule.rationale: Tool-selection accuracy is the primary metric. At tool counts < 25, split wins on accuracy and human readability. At > 25, bundling reduces token noise; but quality loss from confusion outweighs savings unless the bundle is high-confidence.

rule.statement: Follow the decision procedure: (1) List all tools. (2) Count → if < 20, default to split; if > 30, must consider bundling. (3) Group: which tools share an audience AND most arguments? (4) For each group of 3+: bundle as one tool with mode arg. (5) For each lone tool: leave split. (6) Validate: write each tool/mode description; if hard to write, you've grouped wrong. (7) Test on a 50-task eval: if quality drops > 2%, revert the bundle.
rule.rationale: This procedure prevents over-bundling and ensures quality. Validation step catches grouping mistakes early.

rule.statement: Mode arg MUST be enum-constrained. Mode values are semantic, not codes. Each mode has its own description line. If mode selection error rises > 2%, refine mode enum values (make them more distinct) and tighten per-mode descriptions. Include explicit "do NOT use" lines to differentiate modes. Example: "use read for fetching file content (< 1MB); do NOT use read for non-existent files (returns error); use list to check file existence first."
rule.rationale: Enum constraint prevents invalid mode calls (0% invalid calls vs ~12% with free-string mode). Semantic values are easier for the model to recall. Per-mode descriptions reduce confusion between similar modes.
